name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
what_it_means: API fetches a user-supplied URL without strict validation, allowing requests to internal networks, metadata services, or localhost.
animated_example: Handing your company courier a note that secretly tells them to enter restricted buildings for you.
quick_checks: Allowlist of outbound domains; block RFC1918/localhost/metadata IPs
Restrict schemes to https/http; disable redirects; DNS pinning
Force egress through filtering proxy; validate MIME types
No raw upstream responses reflected to clients
how_to_test: file://, ftp:// schemes; redirect chains to internal IPs
expected_outcomes: Policy error; no internal responses; outbound only via proxy
mitigation: Enforce a strict allowlist of permitted outbound domains and IP ranges at the network layer; explicitly block RFC1918 private ranges, loopback addresses (127.0.0.1, ::1), and cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254)
Route all server-initiated outbound requests through a hardened egress proxy that enforces the allowlist, logs every call with target and actor, and blocks unlisted destinations
Restrict permitted URL schemes to HTTPS only; disable automatic redirect following; re-validate the resolved IP against the allowlist after DNS resolution to prevent DNS rebinding attacks
Never reflect raw upstream responses directly to API clients — parse the response through a strict schema and re-serialize only expected fields before returning data to callers
